content.text: A GenAI programme without phase gates is a funding commitment without accountability checkpoints. Adoption phases give the steering committee structured decision points — advance, pivot, or pause — rather than treating GenAI as a continuous spend with no defined return milestones. This sub-subtopic covers a four-phase model applicable to most enterprises, the milestone criteria for each phase gate, and the planning machinery needed to execute the roadmap.

attrs.alt: Enterprise GenAI adoption phases and gate model

content.text: Why Phased Adoption Matters

GenAI programmes fail most often not because the technology does not work but because the organisation scales before it has the governance, talent, and infrastructure to support scale. Phasing adoption forces the programme to build foundations before scaling — platform reliability, risk controls, and CoE staffing must all be in place before 30 BU teams are simultaneously deploying LLM-powered features.

Phases also create natural checkpoints for the CFO and CTO. A programme that can show 'we delivered $2.1M ARR impact across 3 use cases in Phase 2 before requesting Phase 3 budget' is far more fundable than a programme asking for a 3-year budget commitment upfront.
Design principle: every phase must produce a decision artefact (advance/pivot/pause) at its gate, not just a progress report. A gate without a decision is a milestone with no consequence.
Phase 1: Foundation (Months 0–6)

The foundation phase builds the infrastructure, governance, and team the programme will rely on for the next two to three years. Nothing in this phase is user-facing — the output is capability, not product.
Objectives
Establish the CoE: hire or designate the CoE lead, 2–3 platform engineers, and a risk/compliance liaison
Stand up the shared LLM gateway with logging, authentication, and guardrail middleware
Negotiate the enterprise LLM contract (OpenAI, Azure OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google Vertex)
Publish the GenAI risk framework: tier definitions, approval workflow, incident response runbook
Select 1–3 pilot use cases using the use-case scoring model (value × feasibility × risk)
Establish the intake process and backlog so BUs can submit use cases in Phase 2
Key milestones
Milestone
Target Date
Owner
Evidence
CoE staffed to minimum viable headcount (≥3 FTE)
Month 2
CHRO / CoE Lead
Org chart + onboarding records
LLM gateway deployed to staging with auth and logging
Month 3
CoE Platform Lead
Pen test report + gateway API docs
Enterprise LLM contract signed
Month 4
Procurement + CoE Lead
Executed contract with volume tier
GenAI risk framework published and reviewed by Legal
Month 4
CoE Risk Lead + Legal
Signed-off framework document
1–3 pilot use cases selected and scoped
Month 5
CoE Lead + BU sponsors
Pilot charter with success criteria
Intake portal live and accepting BU submissions
Month 6
CoE Program Manager
First 5 BU intake submissions received
Phase 1 gate criteria
The steering committee reviews Phase 1 completion before approving Phase 2 budget. Gate passes when ALL of the following are true:
CoE has ≥ 3 FTE in role (not in hiring pipeline)
LLM gateway is production-ready with documented SLA (99.5% uptime target)
At least one pilot use case has a signed charter with quantified success criteria
Risk framework has been reviewed and signed by Legal and CISO
Enterprise LLM contract is executed
Phase 2: Pilot (Months 4–9)

Phase 2 runs the selected pilots to production and collects the ROI data the programme will use to justify Phase 3 scale investment. 'Production' here means real users, real data, real volume — not a demo or sandbox. The pilot is the first time the platform, governance, and BU delivery capacity are tested under real conditions.
Objectives
Deploy 2–5 use cases to production (not all pilots will succeed — that is expected)
Collect quantified business impact data: time saved, revenue influenced, error rate reduction
Identify platform limitations, governance gaps, and intake bottlenecks under real load
Build BU delivery capacity: at least one BU engineer per pilot BU trained on the platform
Produce the Phase 2 retrospective: what worked, what did not, what to change before scaling
Pilot selection criteria
Pilots should be chosen to maximise learning, not just ROI. An ideal pilot portfolio has:
Criterion
Why It Matters
Example
High value × low risk
Demonstrates ROI quickly; builds stakeholder confidence
Internal document summarisation for legal team
High value × high risk
Tests governance controls under pressure; forces early learning on risk
Customer-facing support bot with guardrails
Cross-BU dependency
Tests shared platform under multi-tenant load; finds integration gaps
Shared embeddings pipeline used by Finance and HR simultaneously
Phase 2 gate criteria
At least 2 use cases are in production with ≥ 4 weeks of production usage data
At least one pilot has documented, quantified business impact (not estimated — measured)
Platform SLA was maintained at ≥ 99.5% across the pilot period
No Tier 1 (critical) risk incidents remain open
BU delivery capacity: at least 2 BU engineers trained and capable of building on the platform independently
Phase 2 retrospective completed and reviewed by steering committee

# Phase 2 gate: pilot outcome tracker
from dataclasses import dataclass
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class PilotRecord:
    use_case_id: str
    name: str
    live_date: date
    measured_impact_usd: float | None   # None = not yet measured
    risk_incidents_open: int
    bu_engineers_trained: int

    def weeks_in_production(self, as_of: date) -> float:
        if self.live_date > as_of:
            return 0.0
        return (as_of - self.live_date).days / 7

def evaluate_phase2_gate(
    pilots: list[PilotRecord],
    platform_sla_pct: float,
    as_of: date,
) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
    issues = []

    # At least 2 pilots in prod for >= 4 weeks
    mature_pilots = [p for p in pilots if p.weeks_in_production(as_of) >= 4]
    if len(mature_pilots) < 2:
        issues.append(f"Only {len(mature_pilots)} pilot(s) have >= 4 weeks in production; need 2")

    # At least 1 has measured impact
    with_impact = [p for p in pilots if p.measured_impact_usd is not None]
    if not with_impact:
        issues.append("No pilot has documented, measured business impact yet")

    # Platform SLA
    if platform_sla_pct < 99.5:
        issues.append(f"Platform SLA {platform_sla_pct:.1f}% < 99.5% target during pilot period")

    # Open critical incidents
    open_critical = sum(p.risk_incidents_open for p in pilots)
    if open_critical > 0:
        issues.append(f"{open_critical} open risk incident(s) must be resolved before advancing")

    # BU engineering capacity
    total_trained = sum(p.bu_engineers_trained for p in pilots)
    if total_trained < 2:
        issues.append(f"Only {total_trained} BU engineer(s) trained; need at least 2")

    return len(issues) == 0, issues

# Example
pilots = [
    PilotRecord("uc-001", "Legal Doc Summariser", date(2025, 1, 15), 480_000, 0, 2),
    PilotRecord("uc-002", "Support Bot",          date(2025, 2,  1), None,    1, 1),
    PilotRecord("uc-003", "HR Q&A Agent",         date(2025, 3, 10), 120_000, 0, 2),
]
passed, issues = evaluate_phase2_gate(pilots, platform_sla_pct=99.7, as_of=date(2025, 4, 1))
print("Gate passed:", passed)
for i in issues:
    print(" •", i)
# Gate passed: False
#  • 1 open risk incident(s) must be resolved before advancing
Phase 3: Scale (Months 9–18)

Phase 3 is the highest-risk phase in the programme — this is where most enterprise GenAI programmes either accelerate into a durable capability or stall into a managed pilot portfolio that never achieves strategic impact. The distinguishing characteristic of Phase 3 is that BU intake volume exceeds what CoE can handle through manual review alone.
Objectives
Scale from 2–5 to 10–30 active use cases across all major BUs
Enable self-serve intake for Tier 1 (low-risk) use cases — BUs submit, platform auto-provisions
Stand up the BU AI guild network so CoE is no longer the bottleneck for delivery
Implement cost governance: chargeback or showback by BU, per-use-case token budgets
Establish the fine-tuning and custom model pathway for use cases that require domain adaptation
Publish and operationalise the GenAI metrics dashboard for the steering committee
Self-serve intake architecture

The transition from manual intake review to self-serve is the structural change that makes Phase 3 scale work. Tier 1 (low-risk, internal-only, no PII) use cases are auto-approved via a policy engine; Tier 2 and 3 still require human review.

# Self-serve intake policy engine — Tier 1 auto-approval
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum

class Tier(Enum):
    TIER_1 = 1   # auto-approved
    TIER_2 = 2   # CoE review required (5-day SLA)
    TIER_3 = 3   # steering committee review required

@dataclass
class IntakeSubmission:
    use_case_id: str
    title: str
    user_facing: bool           # exposed to external users?
    processes_pii: bool
    processes_phi: bool         # health information
    output_used_in_decisions: bool  # consequential decisions (credit, employment, etc.)
    regulated_data: bool        # financial, legal, classified
    bu_owner: str

    def classify_tier(self) -> Tier:
        # Tier 3: high-stakes or sensitive
        if self.processes_phi or self.output_used_in_decisions or self.regulated_data:
            return Tier.TIER_3

        # Tier 2: user-facing or PII
        if self.user_facing or self.processes_pii:
            return Tier.TIER_2

        # Tier 1: internal, no PII, non-consequential
        return Tier.TIER_1

    def auto_provision(self) -> dict:
        tier = self.classify_tier()
        if tier != Tier.TIER_1:
            raise ValueError(f"Cannot auto-provision Tier {tier.value} use case — human review required")

        return {
            "namespace": f"{self.bu_owner}/{self.use_case_id}",
            "tier": tier.value,
            "token_budget_monthly": 5_000_000,   # 5M tokens/month for Tier 1
            "guardrails": ["pii-detect", "toxicity"],
            "logging": "standard",
            "audit_frequency": "quarterly",
        }

submission = IntakeSubmission(
    use_case_id="fin-report-summariser",
    title="Finance Report Summariser",
    user_facing=False,
    processes_pii=False,
    processes_phi=False,
    output_used_in_decisions=False,
    regulated_data=False,
    bu_owner="finance",
)
tier = submission.classify_tier()
print(f"Tier: {tier.name}")   # Tier: TIER_1
config = submission.auto_provision()
print(f"Namespace: {config['namespace']}")
# Namespace: finance/fin-report-summariser
Phase 3 gate criteria
≥ 10 use cases in production with active users
Self-serve intake operational: Tier 1 use cases provisioned within 1 business day
BU AI guild network established: at least 1 guild lead per major BU
Chargeback or showback by BU operational (CFO sign-off on cost allocation model)
GenAI metrics dashboard reviewed by steering committee for two consecutive quarters
No Tier 1 risk incidents in the last 60 days
Phase 4: Optimise (Month 18+)

Phase 4 has no defined end date — it is the steady-state operating mode of a mature GenAI programme. The focus shifts from expansion to depth: higher-complexity use cases, domain-adapted models, and programme-level cost optimisation.
Objectives
Fine-tune or adapt base models for high-frequency, high-value domain use cases where prompt engineering has hit its ceiling
Implement token-level cost optimisation: caching, prompt compression, model tier routing (small/large model selection by query complexity)
Build advanced agent capabilities for multi-step workflows that require tool use, memory, and planning
Establish the AI talent pipeline: university partnerships, internal career pathways for AI engineers
Conduct the first programme-level ROI audit: total investment vs total measured business impact
Optimisation Lever
Typical Saving
Implementation Complexity
Semantic caching (cache similar prompts)
15–30% token reduction on high-repetition use cases
Low — deploy a vector similarity cache in front of the gateway
Model tier routing (small model for simple queries)
20–40% cost reduction
Medium — requires query complexity classifier
Prompt compression (remove redundant tokens)
10–20% token reduction
Low — use a prompt optimiser library (e.g., LLMLingua)
LoRA fine-tuning for domain tasks
20–50% accuracy improvement on domain queries
High — requires training data curation, GPU compute, eval pipeline
Batch inference for async workflows
30–60% cost reduction on non-latency-sensitive tasks
Medium — requires job queue and async delivery
Common Planning Failures
These are the failure modes that most commonly derail GenAI adoption roadmaps. Each has a structural cause and a specific mitigation:
Failure Mode
Root Cause
Mitigation
Jumping straight to Phase 3 without Phase 1 foundation
Executive pressure for visible results; underestimating infrastructure complexity
Gate criteria are non-negotiable; steering committee must enforce them even under schedule pressure
Pilot phase never ends (permanent pilot purgatory)
No documented gate criteria; pilots kept running because 'they're not ready to scale'
Set a hard Phase 2 gate date at programme kick-off; pilots that don't pass gate are retired, not extended
Phase 3 intake overwhelms CoE
Self-serve not implemented before scaling; CoE becomes the bottleneck
Self-serve Tier 1 intake is a Phase 3 prerequisite, not a Phase 3 output
ROI not measured (only estimated)
No baseline established before pilot; no instrumentation to measure change
Phase 1 milestone: baseline measurement plan signed off before any pilot begins
Governance not kept current with scale
Risk framework written for 3 pilots, not 30 use cases; new risk classes not addressed
Risk framework review is a Phase 3 gate criterion; schedule quarterly reviews thereafter
Milestone Planning Template
The following code generates a structured programme milestone plan from a start date and phase-gate definitions. Use this as the basis for the programme RAID log and steering committee reporting:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date, timedelta

@dataclass
class PhaseMilestone:
    name: str
    offset_weeks: int    # weeks from phase start
    owner: str
    evidence: str        # what the steering committee needs to see

@dataclass
class Phase:
    name: str
    duration_weeks: int
    milestones: list[PhaseMilestone] = field(default_factory=list)

PHASES = [
    Phase("Foundation", 24, [
        PhaseMilestone("CoE staffed (≥3 FTE)", 8, "CHRO/CoE Lead", "Org chart"),
        PhaseMilestone("LLM gateway prod-ready", 12, "Platform Lead", "SLA doc + pen test"),
        PhaseMilestone("Enterprise LLM contract signed", 16, "Procurement", "Executed contract"),
        PhaseMilestone("Risk framework approved", 16, "Risk Lead + Legal", "Signed framework"),
        PhaseMilestone("Pilot charters signed (≥1)", 20, "CoE Lead", "Charter docs"),
        PhaseMilestone("Intake portal live", 24, "Program Manager", "5 BU submissions"),
    ]),
    Phase("Pilot", 20, [
        PhaseMilestone("First pilot live", 4,  "BU + CoE", "Production traffic logs"),
        PhaseMilestone("Second pilot live", 8, "BU + CoE", "Production traffic logs"),
        PhaseMilestone("Impact data collected (≥1 use case)", 16, "CoE Program Manager", "ROI report"),
        PhaseMilestone("Phase 2 retrospective", 20, "CoE Lead", "Retro document"),
    ]),
    Phase("Scale", 36, [
        PhaseMilestone("Self-serve intake live (Tier 1)", 4,  "Platform Lead", "Auto-provisioning demo"),
        PhaseMilestone("10 use cases in prod", 16, "CoE Program Manager", "Usage dashboard"),
        PhaseMilestone("BU guild network established", 20, "CoE Lead + BU Leads", "Guild roster"),
        PhaseMilestone("Chargeback model operational", 24, "CFO + CoE Lead", "First BU invoice"),
        PhaseMilestone("30 use cases in prod", 36, "CoE Program Manager", "Usage dashboard"),
    ]),
]

def generate_milestone_plan(programme_start: date) -> list[dict]:
    plan = []
    cursor = programme_start
    for phase in PHASES:
        phase_start = cursor
        for ms in phase.milestones:
            target = phase_start + timedelta(weeks=ms.offset_weeks)
            plan.append({
                "phase": phase.name,
                "milestone": ms.name,
                "target_date": target.isoformat(),
                "owner": ms.owner,
                "evidence": ms.evidence,
            })
        cursor = phase_start + timedelta(weeks=phase.duration_weeks)
    return plan

plan = generate_milestone_plan(date(2025, 1, 6))
for row in plan[:5]:
    print(f"[{row['phase']:12}] {row['target_date']}  {row['milestone']}")
# [Foundation   ] 2025-03-03  CoE staffed (>=3 FTE)
# [Foundation   ] 2025-03-31  LLM gateway prod-ready
# [Foundation   ] 2025-04-28  Enterprise LLM contract signed
# [Foundation   ] 2025-04-28  Risk framework approved
# [Foundation   ] 2025-05-26  Pilot charters signed (>=1)
Summary
Four-phase adoption model: Foundation (0–6m) → Pilot (4–9m) → Scale (9–18m) → Optimise (18m+)
Phase gates are non-negotiable: no phase advances without steering committee sign-off on all gate criteria
Phase 1 builds the infrastructure and governance; Phase 2 validates them under real production conditions
Phase 3 requires self-serve intake as a prerequisite, not a deliverable — CoE cannot manually review 30+ BU use cases
Phase 4 shifts focus to depth: model adaptation, cost optimisation, and advanced agent architectures
Common failure: skipping foundation or never exiting pilot — both are prevented by hard gate criteria enforced at steering level
ROI must be measured (not estimated) — establish baselines in Phase 1 and collect impact data in Phase 2 before requesting Phase 3 budget
